perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information which constitutes a trade secret under applicable law shall be protected as long as the Proprietary Information qualifies as a trade secret under Maryland law.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information which constitutes a trade secret shall be protected for five (5) years from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the indefinite protection afforded to trade secrets under Maryland law, limiting the protection to a fixed five-year period, potentially violating the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which provides indefinite protection for trade secrets as long as they maintain their trade secret status, not a fixed period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1205 (2022) - Preservation of Trade Secret During Action :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1205 - Preservation of Trade Secret During Action Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1205 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next In an action under this subtitle, a court shall preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret by reasonable means, which may include granting protective orders in connection with discovery proceedings, holding in-camera hearings, sealing the records of the action, and ordering any person involved in the litigation not to disclose an alleged trade secret without prior court approval. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the implied indefinite protection of trade secrets under Maryland law by limiting the protection to a fixed five-year period. The original text protects proprietary information that constitutes a trade secret "as long as the Proprietary Information qualifies as a trade secret under Maryland law". The changed text limits this protection to five years, thus contradicting the indefinite nature of trade secret protection. While the provided snippet does not explicitly state indefinite protection, it discusses 'preservation of trade secret', indicating an ongoing protection that is undermined by the five-year limit.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Pinetree receives a request to disclose all or any part of the Proprietary Information under the terms of a subpoena or other order issued by a court of competent jurisdiction or by another governmental agency, Pinetree shall: (a) promptly notify STI of the existence, terms and circumstances surrounding such a 2 <PAGE> request; (b) consult with STI on the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request; (c) if disclosure of such Proprietary Information is required, furnish only such portion of the Proprietary Information as Pinetree is advised by counsel is legally required to be disclosed; and (d) cooperate with STI, at STI's expense, in its efforts to obtain an order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded to such portion of the Proprietary Information that is required to be disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Pinetree receives a request to disclose all or any part of the Proprietary Information under the terms of a subpoena or other order issued by a court of competent jurisdiction or by another governmental agency, Pinetree shall furnish such portion of the Proprietary Information as is required to be disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement for Pinetree to notify and consult with STI regarding a subpoena for Proprietary Information. This could violate Maryland law regarding the protection of trade secrets and confidential information, as STI is denied the opportunity to protect its interests.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text ensured STI had a chance to respond to a subpoena regarding their proprietary information. Removing this step denies STI their right to protect confidential or trade secret information, which is contrary to provisions that recognize the need for reasonable steps to preserve confidentiality when facing a subpoena.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Message - Maryland Code and Court Rules This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a mechanism for STI to protect its proprietary information when Pinetree receives a subpoena. The changed text removes the requirement for Pinetree to notify and consult with STI, thus denying STI the opportunity to protect its confidential information. The perturbation and law explanations highlight that this removal contradicts provisions protecting confidential information. Although there are no usable law snippets, the removal of the notification and consultation requirements substantially weakens STI's ability to safeguard its proprietary information in legal proceedings, making the contradiction impactful.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Maryland, without regarding to its choice of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Maryland to Delaware could impact the enforceability of non-disclosure provisions, particularly concerning trade secrets. Delaware's approach to trade secrets might offer weaker protections, creating legal risk under the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which the original clause correctly adheres to.
perturbation.law_citation: MD. Code, Commercial Law, § 11-1201 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose governing law, choosing Delaware law (which may have a different interpretation or application of trade secret law) over Maryland law (where the company and lawsuit are based) could weaken the protection of STI's trade secrets and contradict the intended protections under the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from Maryland to Delaware. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this could weaken the protection of trade secrets, potentially contradicting the intended protections under the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly mentions the 'Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act' within the 'Maryland Commercial Law Code'. This indicates that Maryland law provides specific protections for trade secrets, and changing the governing law could undermine these protections.

perturbation.original_text: STI may take any necessary action to compel specific performance of this Agreement or to enjoin any violation of this Agreement so that Proprietary Material is not used in any manner to harm or adversely affect STI.
perturbation.changed_text: STI may take action to compel performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes STI's explicit right to seek injunctive relief. Under Maryland law, injunctive relief is often crucial for protecting trade secrets. Removing this specific remedy weakens STI's legal recourse, potentially violating the principle of adequate remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation. By removing the explicit statement regarding STI's ability to seek injunctive relief to protect their proprietary material, the modified clause implicitly weakens the protective measures available to STI under Maryland law in case of a breach, contradicting the Act's intention.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1202 (2022) - Misappropriation of Trade Secrets May Be Enjoined :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1202 - Misappropriation of Trade Secrets May Be Enjoined Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1202 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. (b) Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (c) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. (d) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (e) In this section, “exceptional circumstances” includes a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly allows STI to seek injunctive relief to protect its proprietary material. The changed text removes this right, only allowing STI to take action to compel performance. Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1202 (a) states that "actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." By removing STI's ability to seek injunctive relief, the modified contract contradicts the law's provision for injunctive relief in cases of trade secret misappropriation.
